curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Investigate and explain the constant relationship between circumference and diameter, interpret the gradient of a C–d graph as π, and use C = πd (or C = 2πr) to solve and communicate real-world circle problems.

verse_ality.driving_question: Why does the circumference of a circle always scale in a constant way with its diameter — and what does that tell us about π?

knowing_world.focus: Define circumference and diameter, measure real objects, explore the straight-line relationship between C and d, and formalise the rule C = πd (and C = 2πr).
key_concepts: C–d graph is a straight line
activities: Measure the diameter of a lid/cup.
Wrap string around the edge to measure circumference.
Compute C ÷ d and notice the value stays close to π.
Plot circumference (C) against diameter (d) and interpret the gradient.

human_story.focus: Explore how π has fascinated civilisations for thousands of years and why it acts as a bridge between practical measurement, art, science and mystery.

expressive_self.focus: Make meaning of π and the C–d relationship through visual and spoken expression using real circular objects.
activities: Create a visual diagram showing how circumference changes as diameter increases.
Record a short voice note: “What does π mean to me?”
Make a collage of circular objects labelled with diameter and estimated circumference.

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive tool to resize circles and observe how the C–d graph behaves, then write a rule linking circumference and diameter using the gradient idea.
design_challenges: Try different circle sizes and record diameter and circumference values.
Describe the shape of the C–d graph.
Estimate the gradient of the line and interpret it.
Write a rule that links C and d and test it on new circle sizes.

regulation_supports: Find a circular object and measure its diameter.
Use string to measure its circumference.
Make a circle motion in the air with your arms and imagine stretching it into a line.

pathways.name: Final Task: Circumference–Diameter Poster or Slide
pathways.focus: Create a poster or slide that explains the connection between diameter and circumference using measurements, a graph, and clear communication.
products: Explanation in your own words of the relationship between circumference and diameter.
Diagrams or photos with annotations (include at least one real-life circular object).
A C–d graph (or clearly described straight-line relationship) with a note about the gradient.
Reflection responses: what surprised you, what the gradient tells you, and whether your method could estimate π.

required: Basic measuring skills (using a ruler/tape for diameter and string for circumference)
Division to compute a ratio (C ÷ d)
Reading or sketching simple graphs and understanding gradient as ‘rate of change’

builds_toward: Circle perimeter and area formulas (A = πr²)
Estimation and experimental error in measurement
